content.rendered:
We know Bruce is a Jimmy Cliff fan–his cover of Jimmy’s “Trapped” has been a fan favorite since its debut.

But “Trapped” isn’t the only Jimmy Cliff song Bruce has covered over the years–during World Tour 1993, Bruce frequently covered “Many Rivers to Cross” as well. And while Bruce’s “Trapped” cover was a departure from the original, for “Many Rivers to Cross,” Bruce chose a more faithful arrangement.

Here’s Jimmy’s original 1969 version, written and recorded at the age of 21. It’s the song of a frustrated would-be artist striving to keep his faith in himself and his will to persist, even when those around him abandon him and basic subsistence becomes a struggle.

Bruce’s version retains the soulfulness of Jimmy’s original arrangement–if anything, he ratchets it up, adding a gospel element with his backing singers.

Interestingly, though, Bruce alters the lyrics–most notably by completely dropping the final verse in which the protagonist is so desperate that he’s tempted into “committing some dreadful crime.” Bruce also changes the narrator’s reaction to his woman leaving him from “Well, I guess I have to try” to “All I can do now is cry.”

The result of the lyrical changes: a more traditional ballad of lost love than a down-on-his-luck musician. Why make the change? Who knows–maybe it was more in keeping with the relationships theme of the 1992-93 tour; maybe Bruce felt he’d have more authenticity this way. Or maybe this aspect of the song resonated more with him.

Regardless, it remains a World Tour 1993 highlight. Bruce played it only one time since, at SxSW in 2012 with the original artist. Here’s Bruce and Jimmy performing it together (with the original lyrics) for the first and only time.

Many Rivers to Cross
First performed: April 3, 1994 (Dortmund, German)
Last performed: March 15, 2012 (Austin, TX)

© July 28. 2018

excerpt.rendered: “Many Rivers to Cross” may not be Bruce’s most well-known Jimmy Cliff cover, but it may be his most faithful.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Time Will Tell
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Bruce and Jimmy Cliff team up on the lead-off track from Jimmy's 1969 self-titled album at Bruce's World Tour 1993 show in Verona.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me: Trapped
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: A chance find in an airport duty-free store led to a new Bruce arrangement of an obscure Jimmy Cliff song--and an instant fan favorite.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me: Satan’s Jeweled Crown
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: In the spring of 1993, Bruce leaned further into his new band's strengths with their gorgeous cover of a country-gospel classic.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me, Cleveland School of Rock: Trapped
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: If you ever wonder whether Bruce's music will stay relevant to new generations, watch this clip of the Cleveland School of Rock performing "Trapped" and put your mind at ease.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Whip My Hair
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Bruce and Jimmy Fallon (as Neil Young) perform the unlikeliest of covers in one of Bruce's most viral moments. Watch the performance and backstory inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Jimmy LaFave: Land of Hope and Dreams
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Jimmy LaFave's cover of Bruce's "Land of Hope and Dreams" is more tender than the original--less anthemic, but equally idealistic.
